figures.caption: Figure 2. Log Scaled Performance Improvement of PQN with ABS relative to vanilla PQN on the Full ALE benchmark. Notably, ABS improves PQN performance in most environments.
Figure 8. Log Scaled Performance Improvement of PQN-L with large fixed batches and ABS over various Lmax relative to vanilla PQN-L on the Atari-10 benchmark.
Figure 7. Log Scaled Performance Improvement of PQN with ABS over various Lmax relative to vanilla PQN on the Atari-10 benchmark.
Figure 9. Log Scaled Performance Improvement of PQN-XL with large fixed batches and ABS over various Lmax relative to vanilla PQN-XL on the Atari-10 benchmark.
Figure 3. Learning Curves of Vanilla PQN, with ABS(Ours) and GNS on Atari-10. ABS (ours) boosts the overall performance of PQN across most tasks, surpassing both vanilla PQN and GNS in efficiency.

Figure 4. Mean Improvement Rate of ABS over each Baseline (Left: PQN, Center: PQN-L and Right: PQN-XL) on Atari-10, as a function of Rollout Range. The improvement rate consistently grows with model capacity and with larger Lmax (maximum rollout length; batch size), demonstrating a positive correlation between model scale and the efficacy of adaptive batch.
Figure 5. Log Scaled Performance Improvement of PQN with ABS over various Hyperparameter settings (Top: Rollout Range, Center: Policy Change Thresholds and Bottom: Adapt Frequency) on Atari-10.
Table 4. Early (first 10%) and Late (last 10%) training performance across 10 environments.
